summary: Smoking Help‑Slip‑Franklin's Tower and soaring Terrapin jam
blurb: The show opens with a blistering Help On The Way → Slipknot → Franklin’s Tower that sets the night on fire. The second set delivers an epic Terrapin Station into The Other One and a hair‑raising Morning Dew, punctuated by tight interplay and laughing drums. Highlights also include Peggy‑O, Brown Eyed Women and a rousing encore.
review: July 18, 1990 at Deer Creek Music Center captured one of the high points of the ’90 summer trek. From the opening trio—Help On The Way into Slipknot and Franklin’s Tower—the band was locked in, with Jerry’s guitar blazing and Brent’s keyboards adding a ballistic edge. The second set built momentum through a fiery China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider segue, then exploded on Terrapin Station, a rolling jam, and the thunderous transition into The Other One that culminated in an unforgettable Morning Dew. Throughout the night, Mickey and Billy supplied tribal drum rolls and their trademark laughing drums, while Phil’s bass provided a deep, thunder‑like charge. Even with occasional slip-ups—Deal in the first set and a flat moment on Cassidy—the overall performance is praised as “on fire” by multiple listeners, making this show a must‑listen for anyone chasing that late‑summer Dead magic.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Across more than a dozen recordings the sentiment is overwhelmingly positive: descriptors like “legendary”, “smoking Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s”, “epic Terrapin/Other One jams”, and “hair‑raising Morning Dew” appear in multiple analyses. Only one recording notes mixed feelings, but the majority of reviewers celebrate the performance, giving a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent matrix capture with crisp, balanced sound that blends board clarity and audience ambience, preserving the show’s energy and detail.

key_highlights: Blazing Help On The Way → Slipknot → Franklin’s Tower sequence opens the night.
Terrapin Station jam flows into The Other One and a soaring Morning Dew climax.
Peggy‑O and Brown Eyed Women stand out as tight, vocal‑driven moments.
Mickey and Billy’s laughing drums add playful, tribal texture throughout.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers powerful, emotive solos—especially on the opening Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s trio and the climactic Morning Dew—often described as “on fire” despite occasional haggard moments.
band_performance.Phil: Provides a thunderous, deep bass foundation; leads The Other One with a charge melody and maintains tight interplay throughout, even when brief mix dips are noted.
band_performance.Bob: Offers solid rhythm guitar work and reliable vocal delivery, anchoring the set without any major criticism.
band_performance.Brent: Shows ballistic keyboard work—sweet intros on Easy To Love You, cascading B3 on Morning Dew, and memorable solos on Cassidy—though a few recordings note his level sits high in the mix or sounds a bit tired at times.
band_performance.Mickey: Adds tribal drum rolls, laughing drums, and an intense double‑time feel that drives the second‑set energy.
band_performance.Billy: Locks in with Mickey, supplying steady propulsion and contributing to the playful “evil laughing drums” vibe.
